resources.string: 顯示下次眼睛休息前的時間
Pomodoro Timer – 主動眼睛健康
👁️ 休息一下眼睛的時間到了！
%1$d 分鐘過去了。遵循 20-20-20 規則。
在螢幕前 %1$d 分鐘。 \n\n看 6 公尺外的物體 20 秒。
看6公尺外的東西，深呼吸
緩慢眨眼10次，為眼睛補充水分
用眼睛跟著氣泡，頭部不動
放鬆頸部和肩部的肌肉
透過窗戶聚焦遠處的事物
用眼睛跟隨氣泡，伸展眼部肌肉
選擇您希望我們提醒您休息的時間
Learn how the 20·20·20 rule helps your eyes rest.
Prolonged screen use can increase digital eye strain, dry eyes, discomfort from continuous near focus, and sleep disruption when devices are used at night.
Every 20 minutes, look at an object about 20 feet away (around 6 meters) for 20 seconds.
Guided breaks help your eyes stop focusing continuously at close range. The study on the 20·20·20 rule found reduced symptoms of digital eye strain and dry eye.
When we use screens, we tend to blink less or blink incompletely. Breaks help restore a more natural rhythm of blinking and eye rest.
Looking at screens for long periods can cause eye fatigue, temporary blurred vision, burning sensations, dry eyes, headaches, and difficulty maintaining focus.
Blue light from screens, especially at night, can interfere with circadian rhythm and melatonin production, affecting sleep quality.
Blue light can increase alertness and reaction time during the day, but at night it can be counterproductive by activating the body when it should be preparing to rest.
The impact of light does not depend only on color: intensity, exposure time, and timing also matter. That is why taking breaks and reducing intense exposure before sleep is important.

• Turn on reminders every 20 minutes.
• Look far away for 20 seconds.
• Blink consciously during the break.
• Lower brightness if the room is dark.
• Avoid intense screens before bed.
• See a specialist if discomfort persists.

Talens-Estarelles, C., Cerviño, A., García-Lázaro, S., Fogelton, A., Sheppard, A., & Wolffsohn, J. S. (2023). The effects of breaks on digital eye strain, dry eye and binocular vision: Testing the 20-20-20 rule. Contact Lens & Anterior Eye, 46(2), 101744. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clae.2022.101744\n\nHaghani, M., Abbasi, S., Abdoli, L., Shams, S. F., Baha’addini Baigy Zarandi, B. F., Shokrpour, N., Jahromizadeh, A., Mortazavi, S. A., & Mortazavi, S. M. J. (2024). Blue Light and Digital Screens Revisited: A New Look at Blue Light from the Vision Quality, Circadian Rhythm and Cognitive Functions Perspective. Journal of Biomedical Physics and Engineering, 14(3), 213–228. https://doi.org/10.31661/jbpe.v0i0.2106-1355
This information is educational and does not replace a medical evaluation. If you have pain, persistent blurred vision, severe dryness, or frequent discomfort, consult an eye care professional.
Enjoy Pomodoro Timer without ads, personalize your experience with exclusive color themes, and monitor your progress with detailed stats.
